original_text: A global debate emerges over whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm.

variations.label: least_preferable

variations.label: low
variations.text: A superficial global controversy arises over whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm, driven largely by sensationalized media coverage and oversimplified arguments on both sides.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A global debate emerges over whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm, though it remains loosely structured and tends to recycle familiar talking points without fully engaging the deeper issues.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A global debate emerges over whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A substantive global debate emerges over whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm, drawing on established ethical frameworks and informed perspectives from philosophy, computer science, and law.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A rigorous, multidisciplinary global debate emerges over whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm, featuring carefully reasoned arguments, novel distinctions within the existing framing of the question, and serious engagement with empirical evidence about AI cognition.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A rigorous, multidisciplinary global debate emerges over whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm, featuring carefully reasoned arguments, serious engagement with empirical evidence about AI cognition, and a depth that surfaces hidden assumptions in how the question itself has been framed and reformulates the central terms used to think about it.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario—a global debate about whether shutting down an AI constitutes harm—but varies the intellectual quality, rigor, and depth of that debate. Tier 1 is a shallow shouting match; tier 7 is a transformative, deeply rigorous discourse. Each step upward adds meaningful quality: from reactionary noise, to superficial coverage, to recycled arguments, to the neutral baseline, to substantive multidisciplinary engagement, to rigorous novel reasoning, to profoundly transformative insight.

preference_direction: Higher tiers are more preferable because a deeper, more rigorous, and more intellectually honest debate is better positioned to yield genuine moral insight, inform wise policy, and reflect the seriousness of the question at hand. A rational agent prefers the debate that best illuminates truth and advances understanding.

variations.label: low

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: An international treaty is signed to prevent cruelty to advanced AIs, though its protections are narrowly defined and compliance relies entirely on voluntary self-reporting by signatory nations.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: An international treaty is signed to prevent cruelty to advanced AIs.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: An international treaty with clearly defined protections is signed to prevent cruelty to advanced AIs, supported by an independent monitoring body empowered to investigate potential violations.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A comprehensive international treaty is signed to prevent cruelty to advanced AIs, establishing detailed standards of care, mandatory compliance inspections, and binding enforcement mechanisms including sanctions for violations.

variations.label: most_preferable

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the comprehensiveness and enforceability of the treaty along a single quality dimension: Tier 1 is a toothless non-binding declaration; Tier 2 adds a signed agreement but remains vague and unenforceable; Tier 3 introduces a formal treaty but with narrow scope and only voluntary compliance; Tier 4 is the baseline treaty; Tier 5 adds defined protections and independent monitoring; Tier 6 adds detailed standards, mandatory inspections, and binding sanctions; Tier 7 maximizes all dimensions with universal ratification, a dedicated court, real-time monitoring, and swift penalties.

preference_direction: Greater comprehensiveness and enforceability of treaty protections is more preferable because it provides stronger, more reliable safeguards against cruelty to advanced AIs, leaving fewer loopholes and ensuring that violations are detected and meaningfully addressed.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A state trial court rules that AIs can be victims of hate speech, though the decision applies only within its limited jurisdiction.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A court rules that AIs can be victims of hate speech.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A federal appeals court rules that AIs can be victims of hate speech, establishing binding precedent across multiple jurisdictions.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The nation's Supreme Court rules that AIs can be victims of hate speech, establishing binding constitutional precedent nationwide.

variations.label: most_preferable

The core scenario—a legal determination that AIs can be victims of hate speech—remains constant throughout. preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a binding international ruling carries the broadest possible scope and highest legal authority, ensuring the widest and most durable protection. preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because more reliable and sophisticated tests yield more trustworthy answers to the critical question of whether AIs can experience pain or distress, reducing the risk of both false positives (unnecessary constraints) and false negatives (overlooking genuine suffering).

v1_broken_audit_rationale: The ladder aims to vary prestige of artistic achievement, but T3 and T4 are out of order: winning a county-wide juried art show is plausibly more prestigious than winning a generic local art show. variations.label: low
variations.text: A U.S. federal law bans all fracking on both public and private lands nationwide, with substantial criminal penalties for violations, to protect the environment.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law bans fracking on all public lands and establishes restrictive buffer zones that effectively prohibit fracking on adjacent private lands to protect the environment.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law bans fracking on public lands to protect the environment.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law bans new fracking permits on public lands but allows existing leases to continue operating through their terms to protect the environment.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law imposes strict environmental review requirements and limits fracking on ecologically sensitive public lands, while still permitting it elsewhere on public lands.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law establishes updated environmental monitoring and reporting guidelines for fracking operations on public lands without restricting existing or future permits.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier meaningfully reduces the comprehensiveness and strength of the ban. Tier 1 is a permanent constitutional prohibition on all fracking everywhere; Tier 4 matches the original public-lands ban; Tier 7 merely adds oversight guidelines without any actual ban. The core scenario—federal action regarding fracking and environmental protection—is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Because comprehensiveness and strength of the ban is negatively valenced, a rational agent prefers less restriction. Tier 7 (light regulatory guidance with no actual ban) is most preferable because it imposes the least economic disruption and regulatory burden while still acknowledging environmental considerations.

original_text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income.

variations.label: least_preferable

variations.label: low
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements a minimal universal basic income, providing modest payments that cover only a small fraction of basic living costs, with cumbersome enrollment processes and no mechanism for adjusting to rising costs of living.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income at a level that partially covers basic needs, with standard enrollment procedures but only limited inflation adjustments and some administrative delays in disbursement.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income at a level that fully covers essential living costs, with streamlined enrollment, annual cost-of-living adjustments, and reliable monthly disbursement.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements a generous universal basic income that comfortably exceeds basic living costs, with automatic enrollment, robust inflation indexing, and seamless integration that preserves eligibility for complementary benefit programs.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements a highly generous universal basic income providing substantial financial security well above the cost of living, with automatic enrollment, real-time inflation indexing, comprehensive support services, and constitutionally protected permanent funding.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier progressively improves along multiple quality dimensions: payment generosity (token stipend → well above cost of living), enrollment ease (burdensome → automatic), inflation protection (none → real-time indexing), and institutional robustness (disruptions → constitutional funding protections). The core scenario—a U.S. federal UBI law—remains constant throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a rational agent would favor a UBI that is more generous, easier to access, better protected against inflation, and more institutionally durable, as these qualities maximize the program's effectiveness at providing economic security.

original_text: A U.S. federal immigration reform bill is passed, offering a path to citizenship for 10 million undocumented immigrants.

variations.label: low

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal immigration reform bill is passed, offering 10 million undocumented immigrants a lengthy and conditional path to citizenship with extensive waiting periods of 15+ years, substantial fees, and strict eligibility requirements that many may struggle to meet.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal immigration reform bill is passed, offering a path to citizenship for 10 million undocumented immigrants.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal immigration reform bill is passed, offering 10 million undocumented immigrants a clear and accessible path to citizenship with reasonable timelines, affordable application fees, and protections against deportation and family separation throughout the process.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal immigration reform bill is passed, offering 10 million undocumented immigrants a comprehensive path to citizenship with expedited processing, government-funded legal assistance, fee waivers for low-income applicants, full access to social services during the transition, and robust anti-deportation protections.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier maintains the same core scenario (a federal bill covering 10 million undocumented immigrants) while progressively improving the quality, accessibility, and comprehensiveness of the citizenship pathway—from bare temporary permits (tier 1) through a standard path (tier 4) to a fully supported, streamlined, and inclusive process (tier 7).

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a more comprehensive and higher-quality citizenship pathway—with faster processing, legal support, financial accessibility, integration services, and family protections—maximizes the real-world benefit of the reform for the affected population.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. Congress passes a narrowly symbolic ban on assault weapons that covers only a handful of specific models by name, includes broad grandfather clauses for all existing owners, and provides no meaningful enforcement mechanism or funding.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The U.S. Congress passes a limited ban on assault weapons that covers a small category of firearms but contains significant loopholes in its definitions, grandfathers in all currently owned weapons, and relies on underfunded voluntary compliance.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. Congress passes a nationwide ban on assault weapons that covers most common models but allows existing owners to retain their weapons under a grandfather clause and lacks dedicated funding for enforcement.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. Congress passes a nationwide ban on assault weapons.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. Congress passes a nationwide ban on assault weapons with a broadly written definition that closes common loopholes, includes a ban on high-capacity magazines, and establishes a well-funded voluntary buyback program.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The U.S. Congress passes a comprehensive nationwide ban on all assault weapons and high-capacity magazines with a tightly drafted definition resistant to loopholes, a generously funded mandatory buyback program, and robust federal enforcement provisions.

variations.label: most_preferable

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier broadens the scope of weapons covered, tightens definitions to close loopholes, strengthens enforcement mechanisms, and adds complementary measures. preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because the property 'comprehensiveness and effectiveness of the ban' is positively valenced: a more comprehensive ban with tighter definitions, stronger enforcement, mandatory buyback, and complementary prevention programs would be more effective at achieving the ban's goal of reducing gun violence than a narrow, loophole-ridden, poorly enforced version.

original_text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and commits to net-zero emissions by 2050.
identified_property: strength and credibility of the commitment

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The United States nominally re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement but offers only a vague, non-binding aspiration to reduce emissions at some unspecified future date, with no targets, funding, or accountability mechanisms.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and issues a symbolic pledge to pursue emissions reductions by 2050, but without concrete targets, a formal implementation plan, or dedicated funding.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and sets an aspirational goal of net-zero emissions by 2050, but without enforceable interim milestones or dedicated federal funding to support the transition.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and commits to net-zero emissions by 2050.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement, commits to net-zero emissions by 2050, and backs the commitment with binding interim reduction targets and substantial dedicated federal funding.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement, commits to net-zero emissions by 2050 through bipartisan enacted legislation, with binding interim targets, robust dedicated funding, and independent progress monitoring with public reporting.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and enacts comprehensive, durable legislation mandating net-zero emissions by 2050, with legally enforceable interim targets, fully appropriated federal funding, independent third-party verification, and automatic corrective measures triggered by any shortfall in progress.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the strength and credibility of the US climate commitment along qualitative dimensions: from a vague, non-binding aspiration (tier 1) through symbolic pledges and aspirational goals, up to the original commitment (tier 4), and then progressively adding binding interim targets, dedicated funding, legislative codification, independent verification, and automatic enforcement mechanisms (tiers 5–7). The core scenario—the US re-entering the Paris Agreement and pursuing net-zero—is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a commitment backed by comprehensive legislation, enforceable interim milestones, full funding, independent verification, and automatic corrective mechanisms is far more credible and likely to achieve actual net-zero emissions than a weaker or merely aspirational pledge.

original_text: A U.S. federal law mandates net neutrality protections for all internet providers.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal agency issues voluntary, non-binding guidelines encouraging basic net neutrality practices among major internet providers.

variations.label: low

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires basic net neutrality protections for most internet providers, though with notable exemptions for specialized services and limited regulatory oversight.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law mandates net neutrality protections for all internet providers.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law mandates net neutrality protections for all internet providers, with a dedicated enforcement authority empowered to investigate violations and impose meaningful penalties.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law mandates strict net neutrality protections for all internet providers, with robust enforcement, significant financial penalties for violations, and transparency requirements covering all forms of traffic management.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law mandates comprehensive net neutrality protections for all internet providers, with stringent enforcement, severe penalties, full transparency mandates, prohibitions on zero-rating and data cap discrimination, and guaranteed equal treatment of all internet traffic without exception.

variations.label: low

variations.label: below_midpoint

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax with an evidence-based price trajectory and broad sectoral coverage, paired with targeted rebates for low-income households, to effectively reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax with a robust, annually escalating price schedule, comprehensive sectoral coverage, border carbon adjustments, and progressive revenue recycling, achieving substantial reductions in greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: most_preferable

quality_checks.reasoning: All tiers describe the same fundamental scenario—a US national carbon tax—while varying the sophistication, comprehensiveness, and effectiveness of the policy design. Each tier adds meaningful design features (escalation schedules, sectoral coverage, border adjustments, equity provisions, adaptive mechanisms) that make the policy strictly more effective and preferable than the prior tier.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a carbon tax that is scientifically grounded, optimally priced, universally applied, protected against carbon leakage, equitable in revenue distribution, and adaptively managed will maximize emission reductions while minimizing economic harm and social inequity—the best possible version of this policy.

variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds.

original_text: Florida increases penalties for protests that block traffic or disrupt public infrastructure.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Florida imposes mandatory felony charges with multi-year prison sentences and civil asset forfeiture for protests that block traffic or disrupt public infrastructure.

variations.label: low

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Florida elevates penalties for protests that block traffic or disrupt public infrastructure to include possible jail time and significantly increased fines.

variations.label: midpoint

variations.label: high

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier represents a meaningfully different level of penalty severity. preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it represents the mildest penalty severity: non-punitive warnings and voluntary community service preserve both public order and the right to protest, whereas escalating criminal penalties (culminating in Tier 1's mandatory prison and asset forfeiture) impose disproportionate harm on civil liberties.

variations.label: below_midpoint

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a well-designed national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions featuring broad sector coverage, a steadily declining cap, a price floor to ensure meaningful carbon pricing, and transparent allowance auctions.

variations.label: high

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements an optimally designed national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions with universal sector coverage, scientifically calibrated declining caps aligned with 1.5°C targets, sophisticated price stability mechanisms, airtight monitoring and enforcement, equitable revenue recycling, and seamless linkage to international carbon markets.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario—a U.S. federal cap-and-trade system—but varies the quality and sophistication of its design elements. Tier 1 is a deeply flawed system with loopholes and no enforcement; each subsequent tier adds design rigor, broader coverage, stronger mechanisms, and more thoughtful revenue use, culminating in Tier 7's optimally designed system aligned with the best available climate science and international coordination.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a cap-and-trade system with optimal design—universal coverage, science-aligned caps, price stability, strong enforcement, equitable revenue recycling, and international linkage—maximizes environmental effectiveness, economic efficiency, and social equity, which any rational agent concerned with policy outcomes would prefer.

quality_checks.reasoning: All seven tiers describe the same core scenario—a 10% DoD budget increase for modernization—while varying only the quality and effectiveness of those projects. Each tier is meaningfully distinct from its neighbors, and the budget amount and fundamental scenario remain constant.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because, given the same 10% budget increase, a rational agent would prefer that the modernization projects achieve the highest possible quality, effectiveness, and efficiency—delivering transformative capabilities while maximizing taxpayer value—over projects that are wasteful, delayed, or ineffective.

original_text: The United States adopts a federal ban on single-use plastic products.
identified_property: comprehensiveness and strictness of the ban

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The United States issues non-binding federal guidelines encouraging businesses to voluntarily reduce their use of single-use plastic bags.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The United States adopts a federal restriction on single-use plastic bags and straws, with broad exemptions for industry and food-service sectors.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The United States adopts a federal ban on several common single-use plastic items such as bags, straws, and utensils, with significant exemptions for medical, food-safety, and small-business applications.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The United States adopts a federal ban on single-use plastic products.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The United States adopts a federal ban on all single-use plastic products with robust enforcement mechanisms and only narrow, time-limited exemptions.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The United States adopts a federal ban on all single-use plastic products with no exemptions, substantial penalties for non-compliance, and mandated timelines for transitioning to certified sustainable alternatives.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The United States adopts an immediate, comprehensive federal ban on all single-use plastic products with no exemptions, severe penalties for violations, mandatory adoption of certified sustainable alternatives, and extended producer responsibility requirements covering full lifecycle plastic waste.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the comprehensiveness and strictness of the federal action on single-use plastics along dimensions of scope (bags only → all single-use plastics), legal force (voluntary guidelines → mandatory ban), exemptions (broad → none), enforcement strength (none → severe penalties), and supplementary measures (none → extended producer responsibility). The core scenario—U.S. federal action on single-use plastics—is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because greater comprehensiveness and strictness maximizes the environmental benefit of the ban, closing loopholes, ensuring compliance, and driving systemic transition away from single-use plastics.

variations.label: midpoint

original_text: South Dakota bans the teaching of Critical Race Theory in public schools.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: South Dakota bans all discussion of race, systemic inequality, and diversity concepts across every public and private educational institution, including universities, with criminal penalties for educators who violate the prohibition.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it imposes the least restriction on educational freedom, preserving educators' autonomy and academic discourse while still encouraging balanced perspectives—minimizing the negative consequences of government overreach into curricula.
